summary: The corporate secretary is the officer responsible for maintaining corporate records, organizing board and shareholder meetings, ensuring compliance with filing requirements, and acting as the custodian of the company's minute book. In startups, the role is often filled by a founder, an outside lawyer, or a corporate services provider. The corporate secretary ensures the company keeps its corporate house in order.
whatTheyDo: The corporate secretary prepares and distributes board meeting agendas and materials, records and maintains minutes of directors' and shareholders' meetings, files annual returns and other government filings, maintains the central securities register (share register), keeps custody of the corporate seal (if used), ensures corporate resolutions are properly executed and filed, and manages the minute book. They are often the person who coordinates the administrative side of a financing closing.

legalDuties.description: Maintain the minute book including articles, by-laws, minutes of meetings, resolutions, the central securities register, and director/officer registers.
Ensure timely filing of annual returns, changes of directors, changes of registered office, and other statutory filings with the applicable corporate registry.
Issue notices of meetings, verify quorum, record proceedings, and ensure resolutions are properly passed and documented.
Maintain an accurate and up-to-date record of all issued shares, transfers, and security holder details.

qualifications: There are no statutory qualifications to serve as corporate secretary. The role is typically filled by appointment of the board of directors. In small companies, it is common for one of the founders or the company's lawyer to serve as secretary.
commonDocuments: Written resolutions of directors and shareholders
founderNote: The corporate secretary role is often overlooked in early-stage startups, but it becomes critical during due diligence for a financing or acquisition. Investors and their lawyers will ask for a complete and organized minute book. If it does not exist, you will spend significant time and legal fees recreating it. Designate someone to own this role from incorporation. If you use LouisAI, the minute book management features are designed to make this role easier. Common deficiencies found in startup minute books: missing director consents, unsigned resolutions, incomplete securities register, and missing annual filings.
